title: Conservation of biodiversity
summary: Biodiversity is the full range of variation in the living world — across ecosystems, between species, and within the genes of a single species — and it is being lost faster than at almost any time in Earth's history. This lesson builds the whole A4.2 picture: what biodiversity actually means and why it is more than a headcount of species; the evidence for an accelerating, human-driven extinction crisis and its main anthropogenic causes; why biodiversity matters through ecosystem services, resilience and ethical and economic value; and the two great families of conservation — in-situ (protected areas, rewilding) and ex-situ (zoos, seed banks, captive breeding) — with their real strengths and limits. Monitoring and diversity indices are introduced briefly as the way we measure whether any of it is working. Every worked example is laid out the way our marking engine actually awards Paper 2 marks — distinct valid points, named mechanisms and error-carried-forward — so you can see how the way you write an answer protects your score.
learningObjectives: Define biodiversity and distinguish its three levels — ecosystem, species and genetic diversity — explaining why biodiversity is more than a count of species.
Describe the current biodiversity crisis and outline the anthropogenic causes of accelerating extinction: habitat loss, over-exploitation, invasive species, pollution and climate change.
Explain why biodiversity matters, using ecosystem services, ecosystem resilience, and ethical and economic value.
Compare in-situ and ex-situ conservation approaches, giving named examples and evaluating the strengths and limitations of each.
Outline how diversity indices and monitoring are used to measure biodiversity and track the success of conservation.
Present answers so that distinct valid points and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the marks, the way our marking engine awards them.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Variety of Life, and How We Keep It
simpleExplanation.summary: Biodiversity is the variety of life measured at three levels — the range of ecosystems, the number and balance of species within them, and the genetic variation inside each species. Human activity is driving that variety down fast, and conservation is our organised response: either protecting life where it lives (in-situ) or safeguarding it in human care (ex-situ).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of biodiversity like a library. Ecosystem diversity is the number of different sections — history, science, poetry. Species diversity is how many different book titles sit on the shelves and how evenly they are stocked. Genetic diversity is the number of copies and editions of each title, so that if one copy is damaged another survives. A library with only one section, one title and one fragile copy is one accident away from losing everything. In-situ conservation is keeping the library open and protected on its own site; ex-situ conservation is photographing every page and storing the images in a fireproof vault elsewhere in case the building burns.
steps: Recognise that biodiversity has three levels — ecosystem, species and genetic — so a habitat with many species but almost no genetic variation is still fragile.
Identify the human pressures driving the crisis: habitat loss, over-exploitation, invasive species, pollution and climate change.
Justify why it matters — ecosystem services, resilience to disturbance, and ethical and economic value.

Choose and combine conservation methods — in-situ (protected areas, rewilding) to keep whole ecosystems functioning, and ex-situ (seed banks, captive breeding) as a safeguard and a route to reintroduction.
Measure whether it is working by monitoring populations and calculating diversity indices over time.

flashcards.back: The total variety of living organisms, measured at three levels: ecosystem diversity (the range of habitats), species diversity (the number of species AND how evenly individuals are shared among them), and genetic diversity (the variety of alleles within a species). It is not simply a count of species.

ECOSYSTEM diversity — variety of habitats/communities in a region; SPECIES diversity — richness (number of species) combined with evenness (their relative abundance); GENETIC diversity — variety of alleles within a species. Losing any one level reduces overall biodiversity.

Richness is the number of different species present. Evenness is how equal their population sizes are. A wood with 10 species split evenly is more diverse than one where a single species makes up 90% of individuals — even though both have the same richness.

Genetic diversity is the raw material for natural selection. A population with varied alleles is more likely to contain individuals that can survive disease, parasites or environmental change, so it can adapt and recover. Low genetic diversity (e.g. inbreeding) leaves a population unable to respond and prone to extinction.

Present-day extinction rates are estimated to be tens to hundreds of times higher than the natural 'background' rate and are accelerating. Because the loss is driven by human activity, it is often called the sixth mass extinction — the first with an anthropogenic cause.

Remember HIPPO/the big five: Habitat loss (and fragmentation), Over-exploitation (hunting, fishing, harvesting faster than replacement), Invasive species, Pollution, and Climate change. Habitat loss is generally the single largest cause.

A species introduced (usually by humans) outside its native range that spreads and harms native biodiversity. It has no natural predators, parasites or competitors in the new range, so it out-competes, preys on, or brings disease to native species, driving them down. Example effect: predation on ground-nesting birds by introduced rats.

The benefits humans obtain from ecosystems: provisioning (food, timber, medicines), regulating (pollination, climate and flood regulation, water purification), supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation) and cultural (recreation, aesthetic and spiritual value).

The ability of an ecosystem to resist disturbance and recover afterwards. Higher biodiversity tends to increase resilience because functional redundancy — several species doing a similar job — means the ecosystem keeps working even if one species is lost.
Conserving species in their natural habitat, e.g. national parks, nature reserves, marine protected areas and rewilding. Protects whole ecosystems and lets natural selection continue.

Conserving species outside their natural habitat, in human care: zoos and aquaria (often with captive-breeding programmes), botanic gardens and seed banks (e.g. the Svalbard Global Seed Vault). A safeguard for critically endangered species and a source for reintroduction.

A form of in-situ conservation that restores natural processes to a degraded ecosystem — often by reintroducing keystone or apex species (e.g. large herbivores or predators) — so the ecosystem can regulate itself with minimal further management.

A quantitative measure combining richness and evenness. A common form is $D = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{\sum n(n-1)}$, where $N$ is the total number of organisms and $n$ is the number of each species. A HIGHER value indicates greater diversity.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: biodiversity ≠ number of species
flashcards.back: Defining biodiversity as 'the number of species' throws away marks. It is variety at THREE levels (ecosystem, species, genetic), and species diversity itself needs BOTH richness and evenness.

In-situ = in the natural habitat (parks, reserves, rewilding). Ex-situ = removed to human care (zoos, seed banks, captive breeding). A seed bank is ex-situ; a national park is in-situ. Muddling the two is a classic lost mark.

options: The total number of species living in an area
The variety of life at three levels: ecosystem, species and genetic diversity
The number of individual organisms in a habitat
The number of national parks protecting a region
quickCheck.answer: The variety of life at three levels: ecosystem, species and genetic diversity

quickCheck.prompt: A botanic garden storing seeds from endangered plants, and a national park protecting a rainforest, are respectively examples of:
options: Both in-situ conservation
Ex-situ conservation and in-situ conservation
In-situ conservation and ex-situ conservation
Both ex-situ conservation

quickCheck.prompt: Why does an introduced invasive species often cause native species to decline?
options: It has no natural predators, parasites or competitors in the new range, so it out-competes or preys on native species
It always has lower genetic diversity than native species
It is protected by CITES and cannot be removed
It only survives in seed banks and zoos
quickCheck.answer: It has no natural predators, parasites or competitors in the new range, so it out-competes or preys on native species

quickCheck.prompt: Two woodlands each contain 8 tree species. In woodland X the trees are shared roughly evenly among the species; in woodland Y a single species makes up 92% of the trees. Which has higher species diversity, and why?
options: Woodland Y, because it has more of one useful species
They are identical, because biodiversity is just the number of species
Woodland X, because it has the same richness but far greater evenness
Woodland Y, because dominance by one species increases stability
quickCheck.answer: Woodland X, because it has the same richness but far greater evenness

quickCheck.prompt: Why does low genetic diversity make a species more vulnerable to extinction?
options: It increases the number of species in the ecosystem
There is little variation for natural selection to act on, so the population is less able to adapt to disease or environmental change
It automatically makes the species invasive elsewhere
It raises the value of the Simpson's diversity index
quickCheck.answer: There is little variation for natural selection to act on, so the population is less able to adapt to disease or environmental change

faq.q: Isn't biodiversity just the number of species in an area?
faq.a: No — that is the single most common misconception. Biodiversity is variety at three levels. Ecosystem diversity is the range of different habitats and communities in a region. Species diversity combines richness (how many species) with evenness (how equally individuals are shared among them), so a community dominated by one species is less diverse than an evenly balanced one with the same number of species. Genetic diversity is the variety of alleles within a species. A rainforest with hundreds of species but almost no genetic variation within each would still be fragile, which is exactly why 'number of species' on its own is an incomplete definition.

faq.q: Why does genetic diversity matter if the species is still alive?
faq.a: Genetic diversity is the raw material of natural selection. When a new disease, parasite or environmental change arrives, a genetically varied population is more likely to contain some individuals whose alleles let them survive and reproduce, so the population can adapt and recover. A population with little genetic variation — for example one reduced to a handful of inbred individuals — has no such reservoir, so a single epidemic or change can wipe it out. This is why small captive-bred populations, even when numbers rise, can remain at risk: rebuilding numbers is not the same as rebuilding genetic diversity.

faq.q: If in-situ conservation is usually better, why bother with ex-situ methods at all?
faq.a: In-situ conservation is generally preferred because it protects the whole ecosystem and lets species keep adapting in the wild, but it cannot always act fast enough. When a species is critically endangered, when its habitat is being destroyed faster than it can be protected, or when numbers are too low to be safe in the wild, ex-situ methods — captive breeding, seed banks — act as an insurance policy and a source of individuals for later reintroduction. The two are complementary: ex-situ buys time and safeguards genetic material, while the long-term aim is almost always to return organisms to a protected in-situ habitat.

faq.q: What is the difference between an invasive species and a native species that simply becomes common?
faq.a: A native species that becomes abundant is still part of the food web it evolved in — predators, parasites and competitors keep it in check. An invasive species has been moved (usually by humans) outside its natural range, where those natural controls are absent. Freed from predators and competitors, it can spread unchecked and harm native biodiversity by out-competing native species for resources, preying on them directly, or introducing new diseases. The key phrase examiners want is 'no natural predators/competitors in the new environment', which explains the mechanism rather than just naming the problem.

faq.q: Do I need to calculate a diversity index in this topic?
faq.a: You should understand what a diversity index does and be able to interpret one, and you may be asked to use a given formula with data. The point examiners care about is conceptual: a diversity index combines richness and evenness into a single number so that communities can be compared objectively and monitored over time, and a higher value means greater diversity. Repeated monitoring using the same index is how conservationists judge whether an intervention is actually increasing biodiversity rather than just relying on impressions.

sections.content: Biodiversity is the variety of life, and right now that variety is shrinking faster than at almost any point in the fossil record. But 'biodiversity' is a richer idea than a species headcount, and 'conservation' is more than putting a fence around a forest. This lesson does three things in order. First it pins down what biodiversity actually is — variation across ecosystems, between species, and within the genes of a single species — because a precise definition is where most exam marks are won or lost. Then it lays out the evidence for a human-driven extinction crisis and the reasons that variety is worth saving. Finally it compares the two great strategies of conservation, in-situ and ex-situ, and shows how we measure whether any of it works. As in every course lesson, the worked answers are set out the way marks are genuinely awarded, because in a topic that mixes definition, explanation and evaluation, HOW you write decides how many marks you keep.
What biodiversity really means: three levels

Biodiversity is the total variety of living organisms, and it is measured at three distinct levels. Ecosystem diversity is the range of different habitats and communities within a region — a landscape of forest, wetland, grassland and river has higher ecosystem diversity than an equal area of uniform farmland. Species diversity describes the variety of species within an ecosystem, and it has two components that must be considered together: species richness (the number of different species present) and species evenness (how equally the individuals are distributed among those species). Genetic diversity is the variety of alleles within a single species, spread across its populations. The single most important idea in this topic is that biodiversity is not just a count of species: a community with many species but heavily dominated by one is less diverse than a balanced one, and a species with no genetic variation is fragile no matter how many individuals it has.

items: **Ecosystem diversity:** the variety of habitats and communities in a region — more habitat types means more niches and more overall variety.
**Species diversity:** richness (number of species) COMBINED with evenness (how equal their abundances are). Both matter — dominance by one species lowers diversity.
**Genetic diversity:** the variety of alleles within a species; the raw material for natural selection and future adaptation.
**Key idea:** biodiversity is variety at all three levels, not simply the number of species.

sections.content: Why genetic diversity is not optional

It is tempting to think a species is 'safe' once its numbers recover, but numbers and genetic diversity are not the same thing. Genetic diversity is the pool of different alleles a species carries, and it is the raw material on which natural selection acts. When conditions change — a new pathogen, a warming climate, a new competitor — a genetically varied population is more likely to contain individuals whose alleles happen to let them survive and reproduce, so the population can adapt and recover. A population squeezed through a bottleneck to a few closely related individuals loses that variation; inbreeding raises the chance of harmful recessive alleles being expressed, and there is little variety left for selection to work with. This is why a captive-bred population can rebuild its numbers yet remain genuinely vulnerable, and why maintaining genetic diversity is a central goal of serious conservation, not an afterthought.
The biodiversity crisis and the evidence for accelerating extinction

Extinction is natural — species have always come and gone at a slow 'background' rate visible in the fossil record. What is happening now is different in scale and cause. Current extinction rates are estimated to be many tens to hundreds of times the background rate and are still climbing, which is why the present episode is often called the sixth mass extinction. The evidence is drawn from several independent lines: the fossil and historical record of documented extinctions, monitoring of wild populations showing steep declines in average abundance, shrinking geographic ranges, and rising numbers of species moved into threatened categories on global conservation assessments such as the IUCN Red List. Crucially, this is the first mass extinction with an anthropogenic cause — it is driven by human activity rather than a volcanic or asteroid event.
Anthropogenic causes

The human pressures on biodiversity are conventionally grouped into five, easily remembered as HIPPO: Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Population/over-exploitation, and Overarching climate change. Each acts through a specific mechanism, and naming that mechanism — not just the cause — is what earns explanation marks.

items: **Habitat loss and fragmentation** — clearing forests, draining wetlands and converting land to agriculture or cities destroys habitat and breaks what remains into isolated patches; small fragments support fewer species and reduce gene flow. This is generally the single largest cause of biodiversity loss.
**Over-exploitation** — harvesting, hunting or fishing a species faster than it can reproduce drives populations down and can cause extinction (e.g. overfishing collapsing fish stocks).

**Invasive species** — species introduced outside their native range spread unchecked because they have no natural predators, parasites or competitors there, and they out-compete, prey on, or bring disease to native species.
**Pollution** — chemicals, plastics, excess nutrients (eutrophication) and other pollutants poison organisms or degrade habitats until native species can no longer survive.

**Climate change** — shifting temperature and rainfall move the conditions species are adapted to; species that cannot migrate or adapt fast enough decline, and events such as coral bleaching destroy whole communities.

sections.content: Why biodiversity matters

The case for conserving biodiversity rests on several distinct arguments, and a strong answer draws on more than one. The first is ecosystem services: functioning ecosystems supply benefits that humans depend on — provisioning services (food, timber, fresh water, and medicines, many of which are derived from wild organisms), regulating services (pollination of crops, climate and flood regulation, water purification, decomposition of waste), supporting services (nutrient cycling and soil formation) and cultural services (recreation, and aesthetic and spiritual value). The second is resilience: more biodiverse ecosystems tend to resist disturbance and recover from it better, partly because functional redundancy — several species performing a similar role — means the system keeps working even if one species is lost. The third combines ethical and economic value: ethically, many argue that species have an intrinsic right to exist and that we have a duty of stewardship to future generations; economically, biodiversity underpins industries such as agriculture, fisheries, tourism and pharmaceuticals, and holds genetic resources whose value may not yet be known.

items: **Ecosystem services:** provisioning (food, medicines, timber), regulating (pollination, climate, water), supporting (nutrient cycling) and cultural (recreation, aesthetic) benefits humans rely on.
**Resilience:** higher biodiversity increases resistance to and recovery from disturbance, through functional redundancy among species.
**Ethical value:** intrinsic right of species to exist; human responsibility of stewardship for future generations.
**Economic value:** underpins agriculture, fisheries, tourism and medicine, and preserves genetic resources of potential future value.

sections.content: Conservation approach 1 — in-situ

In-situ conservation means conserving species in their natural habitat. Its principal form is the protected area — national parks, nature reserves and marine protected areas — where habitats and the communities within them are legally safeguarded from destruction. Wildlife corridors that reconnect fragmented habitats are an in-situ tool too, restoring the gene flow that fragmentation cuts off. A more ambitious form is rewilding: restoring natural processes to a degraded ecosystem, often by reintroducing keystone or apex species, so that the system increasingly regulates itself with minimal ongoing management. The great strength of in-situ conservation is that it protects the entire ecosystem and every interaction within it, not just one target species, and it allows natural selection to continue so populations keep adapting. Its limitation is that the threats — poaching, pollution, invasive species, climate change — do not stop at a reserve boundary, and protection requires funding, enforcement and, sometimes, resolving conflict with local land use.

items: **What it is:** conservation in the natural habitat — national parks, nature reserves, marine protected areas, wildlife corridors, rewilding.
**Strengths:** protects the whole ecosystem and all its interactions; allows natural selection to continue; maintains natural behaviours; can be cost-effective over large areas.
**Limitations:** external threats (poaching, pollution, invasive species, climate change) still reach inside; needs enforcement and funding; may conflict with human land use.

sections.content: Conservation approach 2 — ex-situ

Ex-situ conservation means conserving species outside their natural habitat, in human care. Zoos and aquaria run captive-breeding programmes to raise numbers of endangered animals, coordinating matings between institutions to preserve as much genetic diversity as possible; botanic gardens do the same for plants; and seed banks such as the Svalbard Global Seed Vault store seeds under conditions that keep their genetic material viable for decades or longer. The strengths of ex-situ methods are that they protect individuals from external threats entirely, they safeguard genetic material as an insurance policy against extinction in the wild, and they can supply individuals for reintroduction to restored habitats, alongside a role in research and public education. But the limitations are real: captive populations are usually small, so genetic diversity is hard to maintain and inbreeding is a risk; animals can lose the natural behaviours needed to survive in the wild; the programmes are expensive; and reintroduction often fails if the original threat or habitat has not been restored. Ex-situ is best understood not as a rival to in-situ but as a complement and a last line of defence — the aim is almost always eventual return to a protected wild habitat.

items: **What it is:** conservation outside the natural habitat — zoos and aquaria (captive breeding), botanic gardens, seed banks.
**Strengths:** removes individuals from external threats; safeguards genetic material; provides stock for reintroduction; supports research and education.
**Limitations:** small populations lose genetic diversity / risk inbreeding; loss of natural behaviours; high cost; reintroduction often fails if the habitat or original threat is not addressed.
**Relationship to in-situ:** complementary — a safeguard and a route back to protected wild habitat, not a replacement for it.

sections.content: Measuring biodiversity: indices and monitoring

Conservation needs evidence, not impressions, so biodiversity is quantified and tracked over time. A diversity index condenses both richness and evenness into a single number that allows objective comparison between communities and monitoring of one community across time. A widely used example is Simpson's diversity index, one common form of which is shown below, where a higher value indicates greater diversity. What matters conceptually is that repeated monitoring — regular sampling and recalculation of the index, alongside population counts and habitat surveys — is how conservationists judge whether an intervention such as a new reserve or a reintroduction is genuinely increasing biodiversity rather than merely appearing to.
D = \frac{N(N-1)}{\sum n(n-1)}

Here $N$ is the total number of organisms of all species and $n$ is the number of individuals of a particular species; $\sum n(n-1)$ means you calculate $n(n-1)$ for each species and add the results. Because the index rewards both a high number of species and an even spread of individuals, it captures the difference between a balanced community and one dominated by a single species — the very distinction a plain species count misses. A higher $D$ means greater biodiversity.

sections.question: Two nearby ponds are sampled for aquatic invertebrates. Pond A contains 12, 11, 10, 9 and 8 individuals of five species. Pond B contains 46, 2, 1, 1 individuals of four species. (a) Calculate Simpson's diversity index $D$ for each pond using $D = \frac{N(N-1)}{\sum n(n-1)}$. (b) State which pond is more biodiverse and explain your answer with reference to richness and evenness. [5]
sections.solution:
**(a) Pond A.**
Total $N = 12+11+10+9+8 = 50$, so $N(N-1) = 50 \times 49 = 2450$.  [M1: correct $N$ and $N(N-1)$]
$\sum n(n-1) = (12\times11)+(11\times10)+(10\times9)+(9\times8)+(8\times7)$
$= 132 + 110 + 90 + 72 + 56 = 460$.
$D_A = \dfrac{2450}{460} = 5.3$.  [A1]

**Pond B.**
Total $N = 46+2+1+1 = 50$, so $N(N-1) = 50 \times 49 = 2450$.
$\sum n(n-1) = (46\times45)+(2\times1)+(1\times0)+(1\times0) = 2070 + 2 + 0 + 0 = 2072$.
$D_B = \dfrac{2450}{2072} = 1.2$.  [A1: both index values correct]

**(b) Comparison.**
Pond A is more biodiverse: $D_A = 5.3$ is much higher than $D_B = 1.2$.  [M1: identifies A with reference to the index]
Although both ponds have similar richness (5 vs 4 species) and the same number of individuals, Pond A has high EVENNESS — individuals are shared roughly equally among the species — whereas Pond B is dominated by a single species (46 of 50 individuals), giving very low evenness. The index captures this because dominance makes $\sum n(n-1)$ large, driving $D$ down.  [A1: links the difference to evenness, not just richness]

*Notice the two ponds would look identical to anyone who only counted species. The index reveals what a headcount hides — this is precisely why biodiversity is not just the number of species.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Defining biodiversity as 'the number of species'** — it is variety at THREE levels (ecosystem, species, genetic), and species diversity itself needs richness AND evenness. A one-word definition throws marks away.

**Confusing in-situ and ex-situ** — in-situ is in the natural habitat (parks, reserves, rewilding); ex-situ is in human care (zoos, seed banks, captive breeding). A seed bank is ex-situ; a marine protected area is in-situ.

**Naming a cause without a mechanism** — 'invasive species reduce biodiversity' scores little; you must say WHY, e.g. they have no natural predators/competitors in the new range, so they out-compete or prey on native species.
**Treating recovered numbers as full recovery** — rebuilding population size is not the same as restoring genetic diversity; a large but genetically uniform population is still vulnerable.
**Listing strengths without limitations (or vice versa)** — 'evaluate' and 'compare' questions require BOTH sides for each method; a one-sided answer is capped.
**Vague ecosystem-services claims** — 'nature is useful' scores nothing; name a specific service (pollination of crops, water purification, medicines from wild species) to earn the mark.

**Saying a higher Simpson's index is always 'good'** — a higher value means greater diversity, but some healthy natural ecosystems are naturally low in diversity; the index is most meaningful when comparing similar habitats or one habitat over time.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

A4.2 questions mix definition, explanation and evaluation, and the explanation marks are awarded analytically — each distinct valid point is worth one mark. Method-style points (M) credit a correctly reasoned idea, answer points (A) credit a correctly named example with its mechanism, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means one weak line does not sink the rest of the answer, provided each point stands on its own. Study how every mark below is tied to a specific, named idea rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Outline TWO human activities that reduce biodiversity, and describe ONE in-situ and ONE ex-situ method of conserving biodiversity. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
One human activity that reduces biodiversity is habitat loss: clearing forest for agriculture destroys the habitat, so the species that depended on it decline or are lost. A second is the introduction of invasive species, which have no natural predators or competitors in the new environment and so out-compete or prey on native species, reducing their numbers. An in-situ method of conservation is establishing a national park, which protects the species within their natural habitat and conserves the whole ecosystem and its interactions. An ex-situ method is a captive-breeding programme in a zoo, which increases the numbers of an endangered species in human care while preserving genetic diversity for later reintroduction.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (A):** a correctly named human activity WITH a brief mechanism — e.g. habitat loss → destroys the habitat species depend on. Naming 'habitat loss' alone is weak; the mechanism secures the mark.
- **Point 2 (A):** a SECOND, different named activity with a mechanism — e.g. invasive species → no natural predators/competitors, so they out-compete or prey on native species. Must be genuinely different from Point 1 (two forms of 'pollution' would score once).
- **Point 3 (A):** one named IN-SITU method with what it does — e.g. national park / nature reserve / rewilding → protects species in their natural habitat / conserves the whole ecosystem.
- **Point 4 (A):** one named EX-SITU method with what it does — e.g. captive breeding / seed bank / botanic garden → conserves the species in human care / preserves genetic material for reintroduction.

**Accept equivalents.** Any valid activity (over-exploitation, pollution, climate change) and any valid method in the correct category are credited — the engine marks on the biology, not on matching one 'ideal' word. But the in-situ mark requires an in-situ example and the ex-situ mark requires an ex-situ example; giving two in-situ methods scores only one of those two marks.

**What the engine rejects.** A bare list — 'habitat loss, hunting, national parks, zoos' — with no mechanism or function scores below full marks, because the marks are for explained points, not labels. Naming a method in the wrong category (calling a seed bank 'in-situ') loses that mark.

**ECF and partial credit.** The four points are independent, so a student who mixes up which activity is which, or gets one method wrong, still keeps the marks for the points that are correct. There is no all-or-nothing penalty — each valid, distinct, correctly categorised point stands on its own.

**Bottom line:** hit four DISTINCT, correctly categorised points — two named activities with mechanisms, one named in-situ method with its function, one named ex-situ method with its function — and you have the 4 marks.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, accepting equivalents, applying ECF, and capping at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name each activity AND give its mechanism, name each conservation method AND say what it does, and make sure one method is clearly in-situ and one clearly ex-situ. A list of labels feels complete but earns little; the mark scheme rewards named, explained, correctly categorised points, and showing each one is what protects your score.
Where this leads

The ideas here connect across the whole course. The three levels of biodiversity link back to classification and to the sources of variation in evolution, since genetic diversity is the same allele variation that natural selection acts on. Ecosystem services and resilience build directly on energy flow, nutrient cycling and food-web structure from ecology. And the crisis framing — accelerating, human-driven extinction — reappears in every discussion of climate change and sustainability. Master the habit this topic teaches: define precisely (variety at three levels), explain by mechanism (why each cause or method works), and evaluate both sides (strengths and limitations), and you have a template that answers almost any question the examiners can build from A4.2.
